description: On 1 October 331 BCE, Alexander's Macedonian-Greek army decisively defeated Darius III's numerically superior Achaemenid forces at Gaugamela. Darius fled eastward and was murdered by his satrap Bessus in 330 BCE. The battle ended effective Achaemenid resistance and opened Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia to Macedonian conquest. Alexander subsequently burned Persepolis (330 BCE), destroying the ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid dynasty.
casualties_source: Arrian, Anabasis 3.15 (Persian losses); Diodorus Siculus 17.61 (300,000 Persian dead, almost certainly exaggerated). Modern estimates: Briant (2002) suggests 40,000-90,000 Persian casualties.
ethical_notes: The burning of Persepolis (330 BCE) — whether a drunken act or deliberate policy — destroyed irreplaceable Achaemenid archives, art, and administrative records. Greek sources frame the destruction as revenge for Xerxes' burning of Athens (480 BCE); Persian and modern Iranian historiography treats it as cultural vandalism. Alexander's conquest is often romanticized in Western tradition ('Alexander the Great'); Iranian tradition remembers him as Iskandar-e Gojastak ('the accursed Alexander'). Both framings are present in AtlasPI.

entity_links.notes: Effective end of Achaemenid military resistance; Darius III murdered 330 BCE

sources.citation: Arrian, Anabasis Alexandri, Book III. (Loeb Classical Library edition, trans. P.A. Brunt, 1976).
Briant, P. (2002). From Cyrus to Alexander: A History of the Persian Empire. Trans. P.T. Daniels. Eisenbrauns.

location_name: Hormozdgan, near modern Bandar Abbas, Fars
main_actor: Ardashir I (Ardashir Papakan), founder of the Sasanian dynasty
description: Ardashir I, a local ruler from Estakhr in Fars province claiming descent from the legendary Sasan, defeated the last Arsacid (Parthian) king Artabanus V (Ardavan V) at the Battle of Hormozdgan on 28 April 224 CE. The victory ended the 470-year Arsacid dynasty and established the Sasanian Empire, which would rule Iran until the Arab conquest of 651 CE. Ardashir was crowned shahanshah ('King of Kings') at Ctesiphon.
ethical_notes: Sasanian sources (the Karnamag-i Ardashir-i Papakan, a later romance) frame the transition as legitimate restoration of 'true' Iranian kingship after Arsacid 'decline.' Arsacid-period sources are almost entirely lost; we depend on Sasanian propaganda and later Islamic-era compilations (al-Tabari). The Arsacid perspective on their own overthrow is a known silence in the historical record.

entity_links.notes: End of the Arsacid/Parthian dynasty after 470 years
Sasanian Empire founded; Ardashir crowned shahanshah at Ctesiphon

sources.citation: Daryaee, T. (2009). Sasanian Persia: The Rise and Fall of an Empire. I.B. Tauris.
Wiesehöfer, J. (2001). Ancient Persia from 550 BC to 650 AD. Trans. A. Azodi. I.B. Tauris.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

main_actor: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, commander of the Rashidun Arab army
description: In November 636 CE (15 AH), the Rashidun Arab army under Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas defeated the Sasanian army commanded by Rostam Farrokhzad at al-Qadisiyyah, south of Ctesiphon. The three-day battle ended Sasanian control of Mesopotamia. Ctesiphon fell shortly after, and the Sasanian treasury was captured. The battle opened the Iranian plateau to Arab conquest, which was completed with the death of the last Sasanian shah Yazdegerd III in 651 CE.
casualties_source: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (primary, with typical inflation of enemy losses). Rostam Farrokhzad killed in the battle. Modern estimates vary widely.
ethical_notes: The Arab conquest of Iran (636-651) ended Zoroastrian political sovereignty and began a gradual but coerced Islamization process. Zoroastrian temples were destroyed or converted; the jizya tax on non-Muslims created sustained economic pressure to convert. The Zoroastrian community declined from a majority to a small minority over 300 years. Modern Iranian nationalism (especially under the Pahlavis) framed al-Qadisiyyah as a national catastrophe; Saddam Hussein instrumentalized the same battle name during the Iran-Iraq War (1980-88, 'Qadisiyyat Saddam'), weaponizing Arab-Persian ethnic framing. Both instrumentalizations are documented here.

entity_links.notes: Effective end of Sasanian control of Mesopotamia; dynasty falls by 651
Rashidun caliphate conquers the Sasanian heartland

sources.citation: al-Tabari (d. 923). تاريخ الرسل والملوك (History of the Prophets and Kings), vol. XII. Eng. tr. Y. Friedmann, SUNY Press 1992.
Morony, M. (1984). Iraq after the Muslim Conquest. Princeton University Press.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

main_actor: Allied Deccan Sultanates (Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, Golconda, Bidar)
description: On 23 January 1565, the allied forces of the Deccan Sultanates (Adil Shahi of Bijapur, Nizam Shahi of Ahmadnagar, Qutb Shahi of Golconda, and Barid Shahi of Bidar) defeated the Vijayanagara Empire at the Battle of Talikota (Rakkasa-Tangadi). The Vijayanagara king Aliya Rama Raya was captured and beheaded on the battlefield. The allied armies subsequently sacked Hampi, the Vijayanagara capital, in a destruction lasting months — temples, palaces, and markets were systematically demolished.
casualties_source: Robert Sewell, A Forgotten Empire (1900), drawing on Portuguese sources (Fernao Nuniz, Domingo Paes). Figures likely exaggerated but the scale of destruction at Hampi is archaeologically attested.
ethical_notes: The destruction of Hampi — one of the largest cities in the world at the time, with a population estimated at 500,000 — is sometimes framed in Hindu-nationalist historiography as a purely religious conflict (Muslim vs. Hindu). The reality is more complex: Vijayanagara employed Muslim generals and administrators; the Deccan Sultanates had Hindu vassals and commanders. The alliance was political-territorial, not purely confessional. However, the scale of destruction of Hindu temples at Hampi is undeniable and archaeologically documented. Both the sectarian oversimplification and the denial of religious dimension are distortions.

sources.citation: Sewell, R. (1900). A Forgotten Empire (Vijayanagar): A Contribution to the History of India. Swan Sonnenschein & Co.
Eaton, R. (2005). A Social History of the Deccan, 1300–1761. Cambridge University Press.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

main_actor: Ahmad Shah Durrani (Abdali), founder of the Durrani Afghan Empire
description: On 14 January 1761, the Afghan forces of Ahmad Shah Durrani decisively defeated the Maratha Confederacy's army at the Third Battle of Panipat. The Maratha army, led by Sadashivrao Bhau and Vishwasrao (the Peshwa's son and heir, killed in the battle), suffered catastrophic losses. The defeat ended Maratha aspirations for pan-Indian hegemony and created a power vacuum that the British East India Company would exploit over the following decades.
casualties_source: Sardesai, G.S. (1946). New History of the Marathas, vol. II. Maratha casualties including camp followers may have reached 70,000; Afghan losses ~20,000-25,000.
ethical_notes: The battle was followed by mass enslavement of Maratha camp followers, women, and children by Afghan forces. Afghan cavalry pursuit of fleeing Marathas continued for days. The battle is instrumentalized in modern Indian politics: Hindu nationalists frame it as a Muslim-Hindu conflict (ignoring that Muslim Rohilla and Awadh forces fought on the Afghan side, and the Maratha army included Muslim artilleryman Ibrahim Khan Gardi). The Maratha defeat opened India to British colonial conquest — a consequence neither side intended or foresaw.

entity_links.notes: Catastrophic defeat ending Maratha bid for pan-Indian hegemony

sources.citation: Sardesai, G.S. (1946). New History of the Marathas, vol. II: The Expansion of the Maratha Power. Phoenix Publications.
Roy, K. (2004). India's Historic Battles: From Alexander the Great to Kargil. Permanent Black.

main_actor: Robert Clive, commander of the British East India Company forces
description: On 23 June 1757, Robert Clive's small East India Company force (approximately 3,000, including Indian sepoys) defeated Siraj ud-Daulah, the Nawab of Bengal, at Plassey. The 'battle' was decided less by combat than by the treachery of Mir Jafar, Siraj's commander-in-chief, who had secretly agreed to defect in exchange for the throne. Siraj was captured and killed; Mir Jafar was installed as a puppet nawab. Plassey gave the Company effective control of Bengal's treasury and revenue, financing subsequent British conquests across India.
casualties_source: Clive's dispatches and Orme, R. (1763). History of the Military Transactions of the British Nation in Indostan. Company losses: 22 killed, ~50 wounded. Nawab's army losses minimal due to non-engagement of most units.
ethical_notes: Plassey was less a battle than a corporate coup enabled by bribery and betrayal. Clive's subsequent plunder of Bengal's treasury (estimated at £2.5 million in 1757 currency, equivalent to hundreds of millions today) and the Company's extractive revenue policies directly contributed to the Bengal Famine of 1770 (estimated 10 million dead, ~one-third of Bengal's population). The Company's 'diwani' revenue collection rights obtained in 1765 formalized colonial extraction. Plassey marks the beginning of British colonial domination of the Indian subcontinent — a process that would last 190 years and affect over 300 million people.

sources.citation: Dalrymple, W. (2019). The Anarchy: The Relentless Rise of the East India Company. Bloomsbury.
Orme, R. (1763). A History of the Military Transactions of the British Nation in Indostan from the Year MDCCXLV. Vol. II.

main_actor: Viceroy Lord Lytton, on behalf of Queen Victoria
description: On 1 January 1877, Viceroy Lord Lytton convened the Imperial Assemblage (Delhi Durbar) at which Queen Victoria was proclaimed Empress of India (Kaiser-i-Hind). The ceremony, held on a vast plain north of Delhi, assembled 68 ruling princes and thousands of British and Indian dignitaries. The proclamation formalized British sovereignty over the Indian subcontinent under the Royal Titles Act 1876, replacing the East India Company's patchwork rule (ended in 1858) with direct Crown authority.
ethical_notes: The Durbar was staged during an ongoing famine in Madras and Bombay Presidencies (the Great Famine of 1876-78, estimated 5.5 million dead per the Famine Commission of 1880). Viceroy Lytton explicitly refused to divert resources from the Durbar celebrations to famine relief, arguing that any display of 'weakness' would undermine imperial prestige. The juxtaposition of lavish imperial spectacle and mass starvation was noted by contemporaries (including Indian press) and remains a defining example of colonial callousness. The title 'Kaiser-i-Hind' (a Hindustani-German hybrid) appropriated Mughal imperial symbolism while erasing Mughal sovereignty.

sources.citation: Cohn, B. (1983). 'Representing Authority in Victorian India.' In Hobsbawm, E. & Ranger, T. (eds.), The Invention of Tradition. Cambridge University Press.
Davis, M. (2001). Late Victorian Holocausts: El Niño Famines and the Making of the Third World. Verso.

main_actor: Brigadier-General Reginald Dyer, British Indian Army
description: On 13 April 1919 (Baisakhi day), Brigadier-General Dyer ordered his troops to fire without warning into a crowd of unarmed civilians gathered in Jallianwala Bagh, a walled garden with limited exits in Amritsar. The crowd included men, women, and children attending a peaceful protest against the Rowlatt Act. Troops fired for approximately 10 minutes, expending 1,650 rounds. The official Hunter Commission (1919) counted 379 dead and over 1,200 wounded; Indian estimates (Indian National Congress) placed the death toll at over 1,000.
casualties_source: Hunter Commission Report (1920): 379 killed, 1,200+ wounded. Indian National Congress contemporary estimate: ~1,000+ killed. Sikh historical records suggest up to 1,500.
ethical_notes: Dyer later testified to the Hunter Commission that he intended to produce a 'moral effect' and would have used machine guns if he could have got them into the garden. He was censured but not court-martialed; the Morning Post newspaper raised £26,000 for him as 'the man who saved India.' The massacre radicalized a generation of Indian nationalists — Jawaharlal Nehru, Rabindranath Tagore (who returned his knighthood), and Udham Singh (who assassinated Michael O'Dwyer, the Lieutenant Governor of Punjab, in London in 1940) all cited Jallianwala Bagh as a turning point. The British government expressed 'regret' (Theresa May, 2019) but has never formally apologized. Sikh memory is particularly acute: the massacre occurred during the Baisakhi festival, adjacent to the Harmandir Sahib (Golden Temple).

sources.citation: Report of the Committee Appointed by the Government of India to Investigate the Disturbances in the Punjab (Hunter Commission Report), 1920. Cmd. 681.
Collett, N. (2005). The Butcher of Amritsar: General Reginald Dyer. Hambledon & London.

main_actor: Ruhollah Khomeini and the broad revolutionary coalition (Islamist, leftist, nationalist)
description: The Iranian Revolution (1978-1979) overthrew Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi after months of mass demonstrations, strikes, and escalating violence. The Shah left Iran on 16 January 1979; Ayatollah Khomeini returned from exile on 1 February 1979. After brief armed clashes (9-11 February), the military declared neutrality. The Islamic Republic of Iran was proclaimed on 1 April 1979 following a referendum. The revolution replaced a secular authoritarian monarchy backed by the United States with a theocratic republic governed by velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the jurist).
casualties_source: Abrahamian, E. (2008). A History of Modern Iran. Low estimate: deaths during the revolution itself (1978-79). High estimate includes post-revolution executions (1979-1988), particularly the 1988 prison massacres.
ethical_notes: The revolution was a broad coalition (Islamist, Marxist, liberal-nationalist) that Khomeini's faction subsequently monopolized by violent purge: mass executions of Mojahedin-e Khalq, Tudeh Party, Kurdish separatists, and Baha'is in 1981-88. The 1988 prison massacres (estimated 4,000-30,000 executed per Amnesty International and UN Special Rapporteur) remain officially unacknowledged by the Islamic Republic. The revolution's aftermath includes the hostage crisis (1979-81), the Iran-Iraq War (1980-88, ~500,000 Iranian dead), and ongoing systematic suppression of women's rights, religious minorities (especially Baha'i), ethnic minorities (Kurds, Baluch, Arabs), and political dissent. The Mahsa Amini protests of 2022-23 demonstrated continued popular resistance to theocratic rule.

entity_links.notes: Pahlavi monarchy ended; Shah fled 16 January 1979
Islamic Republic proclaimed 1 April 1979 by referendum

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. (2008). A History of Modern Iran. Cambridge University Press.
Keddie, N. (2003). Modern Iran: Roots and Results of Revolution. Yale University Press.
